item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you mean, but for me it didn't work because it didn't really make me feel anything for the character. I didn't get a sense of him as a person because there's isn't anything from his point of view or direct characterization. Someone telling me how a faceless character was a nice person who died doesn't really evoke any feelings from me and is as effective as just giving me his date of death.

That's why the obit just felt like an explanation for the anomaly, because I didn't feel like there was enough there to incite an empathetic, or even sympathetic, reaction so it just felt like a closing paragraph.

I do like the bit about it being like a Twilight Zone ending, though. I think there's charm in that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoiler
– hide block

The wormhole is how the eye blinks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Henry Bemis

Wait a minute, haven't I heard that name before?

Dr. Serling

Oh.

Fun article, but I'm a little lost on what the wormhole is for. I guess it's supposed to be Hank reading from the past or something? That's my interpretation, at least. +1 because I love the Twilight Zone.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded:
This one was really neat - and the way logs are used in it was done very well, in my opinion.

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

A few of the specific ways this worked well for me:

Description:

The very simple description here - just describing the object and what it does in the basic sense - sets up the rest of the article well. There’s a very solid foundation (no pun intended) here that the logs are then free to build up on in order to tell the story.

Test Logs

The test logs, like I said, build upon the initial description really well and create a good sense of just how weird this anomaly is, presenting a mystery that we as readers really want the answers to. Despite being strange, the behaviour shown in the logs is consistent, showing us that there is a logic behind the way the anomaly works if we can just understand it.

Discovery

And the discovery provides the answer to the mystery - leaving us to go back to the rest of the article with a new understanding of what’s really going on there. I agree that it’s a little cheeky that this is at the end of the article in-universe, but I’m willing to look past that for the sake of the story.

Great article! +1

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to Tanhony or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.
I'm humbled, I'm appreciative, I've been blinking a lot today in honor of this piece and/or because of seasonal allergies

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I echo some of the other posters that the final note is a little too on the nose, but then again, I prefer that over //the other way/ where the article is obfuscated. Regardless, it doesn't detract from the piece so much that I won't upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, no! There was time now!

Honestly, great article. I'd be a hypocrite for saying the late-in-article discovery log is bending the format to serve the narrative, since I do that all the diggidydang time.

Also, I don't think the final note subtracts from the article. Yes, it makes everything plain and obvious, but honestly, not ever article has to be a grand mystery. Some articles exist as a puzzle to be unraveled, others can just tell a story. I thought this was a good story, so +1

P.S. Oh fine one criticism: not a fan of the article title. Too old a well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not too big a fan of the note at the end but this is an otherwise well-done piece.

item.title: Staff Post - Info
item.content:encoded:
Noting that two series of improper tags had to be removed from this article for being incomplete.

Tech team staff look for articles with no tags, so improper tagging makes their job harder. If you aren't familiar with the tagging system, do not tag articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's to do with how long the testing went on in each case (i.e. ones that ended after less than ten iterations don't need more digits) but I hear you and might tweak it for consistency's sake. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s a great SCP, but it somewhat bothers me that some tests end with -001 and some with just -1. But that’s just a nitpick that won’t keep me from upvoting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a fair way to feel about it.

+ show block
– hide block

Just to explain my thinking a bit (your mileage will vary, of course): The obit is meant not to explain the SCP per se, but to add some additional turns of irony — not just that the man liked books, not just that he went blind, but that he died trying to restore his eyesight. It's not that the failed surgery, or Bemis's love of books, "caused" the anomaly; honestly that's not my personal interpretation of the anomaly at all.

I can absolutely understand someone thinking the obit's writing style in particular takes things over the top, but for me it added an important layer of not just pathos but also narrative closure. It also brings it closer to its inspiration; it's exactly the sort of note The Twilight Zone would choose to end on with a story like this.

As I said, your mileage may vary. For the things I liked most about this story, I found the obit to be an important tone to add to a story that otherwise necessarily keeps things tight and clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was on board until the final note. While it makes sense in-universe, it pretty much beats you over the head with what the anomaly is. The article does a good job of hinting at what it is while keeping a sense of mystery to it, but the final note kills the mystery and turns the article rather dull.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Permission to feel these kind of feels, captain?
;_; +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your ability to completely transform a draft still astounds me dude. This is awesome and you should be proud of what it has become. +1

item.title: To Sleep, Perchance to See
item.content:encoded:
For in that sleep of death what dreams may come
When we have shuffled off this mortal coil,
Must give us pause

Thanks to the many folks inside the collapsible for reading many iterations of this and guiding it to a very different, much better place than where it started.

+ show block
– hide block

TheMightyMcB
Dyslexion
gee0765
Weryllium
Popsioak
IronDruid
JDanen

The original eye photo is here.

spoiler-y note
spoiler-y note

Bemis, Serling, and really this whole SCP are homage to "Time Enough At Last," the classic Twilight Zone episode.
